nativeRange.habitat: Shallow bogs, marshes, pond and stream margins; emerges from wet mud and shallow standing water

decorPreferences: strong overhead lighting for compact carpeting

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, large cichlids, or silver dollars

summary: Hydrocotyle verticillata (Whorled Pennywort) is a creeping foreground-to-midground plant with distinctive round, whorl-arranged leaves. It generally favors strong light and added CO2 to stay low and compact.
careNotes: Hydrocotyle verticillata is a creeping aquatic plant native to North and South America and the Caribbean, recognizable by its round, half-dollar-shaped leaves arranged in whorls along trailing runners. Unlike its sister species H. tripartita (the much more compact 'Japan/Mini' Hydrocotyle), H. leucocephala (Brazilian Pennywort), and H. sibthorpioides ('Pennywort'), H. verticillata is distinguished by these distinctive round, whorl-arranged leaves and its strictly trailing growth form. It is generally considered an advanced plant: in low-to-medium light it tends to grow tall and leggy and quickly loses the compact carpeting form many aquarists want. Strong lighting, added CO2, and a nutrient-rich substrate are commonly advised to keep it dense and low — under those conditions it has been reported to spread rapidly as a foreground or midground creeper, and frequent trimming is generally needed. It tolerates a wide pH (around 5.0–7.5) and is unusually cold-tolerant for an aquarium plant, with hobby sources reporting survival down to about 4 °C and an optimum aquarium range around 18–25 °C; the values written here use the recommended aquarium range. The plant grows readily both submerged and emersed and can be cultivated as a marginal or pond plant. Cross-source notes: care level was rated 'easy to moderate' by Buce Plant but 'advanced/difficult' by Tropica, Flowgrow, and Aquasabi — the more specialist sources were given more weight and the schema's 'advanced' value was chosen. Growth rate was described as 'medium' by Tropica but 'fast' or 'moderate to fast' by Buce Plant, Flowgrow, and Aquasabi — 'fast' was adopted as the cross-source consensus. Lighting was given as 'high' (Tropica), 'medium' (Buce Plant, Aquasabi), and 'medium to high' (Flowgrow); 'high' was selected because all sources agree the compact carpeting form is only achieved under strong light. Family was given as 'Apiaceae' by Buce Plant but 'Araliaceae' by Flowgrow — modern (APG) taxonomy places Hydrocotyle in Araliaceae, which is used here. Minimum tank volume of 40 L is a conservative floor for a small planted setup and is not directly stated by any source. The 'category' value 'Creeping Plant' is an informal grouping; the plant is sometimes also sold and used as a floater.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward via vegetative means: separate runners or daughter plants from a healthy mother plant and replant them, or cut the creeping stem and replant individual segments. Splitting dense clumps is also commonly used. The plant produces new shoots along its trailing stem that can be trimmed off anywhere and replanted.

primary.notes: Manifest-supplied URL (4459) redirected to Lilaeopsis brasiliensis; corrected by searching Tropica's plant catalog. Item no. 039.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, pH, lighting, propagation, and growth rate. Rates care 'easy to moderate' and lighting 'medium', which disagrees with the specialist sources used here.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Araliaceae / Apiales), temperature range (4–27 °C, optimum 18–25 °C), pH (5–7.5), kH (0–15), growth rate, and difficulty.

additional.name: Aquasabi
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (4–27 °C), pH (5–7), gH (0–30), kH (0–15), difficulty, growth rate, and cold tolerance.
